claim: Condition is inverted: `elif not (charset_normalizer_version):` should be `elif charset_normalizer_version:` — as written, the charset_normalizer version check only runs when charset_normalizer_version is falsy (causing an AttributeError from calling .split on None) and is skipped when it's actually present, incorrectly falling through to the 'unable to find dependency' warning.

The isinstance check is inverted: when string is already a builtin_str (str), the code calls string.decode(encoding), but str has no decode() method (that only exists on bytes), causing an AttributeError; when string is bytes, it's returned unchanged instead of being decoded.

The condition `if not (TYPE_CHECKING):` is inverted — it should be `if TYPE_CHECKING:` — so all these type aliases/TypedDicts/imports (ParamsType, DataType, BodyType, HeadersType, FilesType, AuthType, RequestKwargs, PreparedRequest/Response/AuthBase/RequestsCookieJar/CaseInsensitiveDict imports, TypeIs, etc.) are invisible to static type checkers (which set TYPE_CHECKING=True, treating this block

The except (ProtocolError, OSError) handler just does `pass` instead of raising ConnectionError, so `resp` is never assigned and the subsequent `return self.build_response(request, resp)` at line 748 raises an UnboundLocalError, silently discarding the original network error.

The condition `if not (isinstance(e, _SSLError))` is inverted: it raises SSLError for exceptions that are NOT _SSLError (e.g. ReadTimeoutError/_InvalidHeader instances get wrongly wrapped as SSLError), while actual _SSLError instances fall through to the `else: raise` and are never wrapped in requests' SSLError.
s_auth = r.headers.get("www-authenticate") has no default value, so if a 4xx response lacks this header, s_auth is None and the subsequent s_auth.lower() call raises AttributeError.

Condition is inverted: `if not (_digest_auth):` sets prep.headers["Authorization"] = _digest_auth only when _digest_auth is falsy/None, instead of setting it when a valid digest header was built, breaking digest auth retry on 401.

The condition is inverted: at runtime (TYPE_CHECKING is False, so `not TYPE_CHECKING` is True) it directly does `import chardet`, which will raise an uncaught ImportError if only charset_normalizer is installed (the common case), while the safe `_resolve_char_detection()` fallback is only used in the TYPE_CHECKING branch that never executes at runtime.

The condition is inverted with `not(...)`: when response is None (the common case, e.g. exceptions raised without a response kwarg), the negated condition becomes True and the code executes `self.request = response.request`, raising AttributeError on NoneType; the original intent (only override self.request from response.request when response is set, request is unset, and response has a request at
The `__main__` guard condition is inverted (`if not (__name__ == "__main__")`), causing `main()` to run whenever the module is imported rather than only when executed as a script.

DecodeError from the raw stream is silently swallowed (pass) instead of being converted to ContentDecodingError, so content decoding failures are hidden instead of surfaced (ContentDecodingError is imported but never used).

Uses subtraction `(pending) - (chunk)` instead of concatenation `pending + chunk` to join the leftover buffer with the next chunk; str/bytes don't support `-`, so this raises TypeError whenever a pending fragment exists in iter_lines.

The condition `if not ("proxies" not in kwargs):` is equivalent to `if "proxies" in kwargs:`, inverting the intended check; it now overwrites the already-merged per-request proxies with `resolve_proxies(request, self.proxies, self.trust_env)` when proxies IS present in kwargs (discarding request-level proxy overrides), and skips setting proxies entirely when it is absent from kwargs (e.g. calling

Elapsed time is computed as `preferred_clock() + start` instead of `preferred_clock() - start`, producing a nonsensical (roughly double-the-current-time) duration instead of the actual request elapsed time.
Using the `-` operator to concatenate strings (`(__doc__ + "\n") - (...)`) is invalid for str operands and will raise a TypeError at import time since `__doc__` is not None (module has a docstring).

In atomic_open's exception handler, the temp file is removed but the exception is swallowed with `pass` instead of being re-raised, silently discarding I/O errors or exceptions from the caller instead of propagating them.

The loop range `range(1, (len(parts)) - 1)` excludes the last element of `parts`, so the segment following the final '%' in the URI is never re-prefixed with '%' or properly decoded, corrupting the last percent-escape sequence in any URI that contains one.
